role_summary: NAACP Jackson Youth Movement secretary; SNCC voter-registration organizer at Tougaloo College (1964-1967); co-organizer of the White Plains, New York Black Panther Party chapter (1967); English professor and poet at Jackson State University.
ai_reading: Adams-Johnson's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17) at a cosine count of 128; the broader cluster includes Euvester Simpson (entry 41, who was beaten with Fannie Lou Hamer at the Winona jail in June 1963), the joint Geraldine Crawford Bennett / Toni Breaux / Willie Elliot Jenkins interview (entry 46), and the Ladner sisters joint interview (entry 75). The cluster is the Mississippi-Jackson-Tougaloo-and-NAACP-Youth cohort that her organizing arc anchors. The novel observation her page surfaces is the cross-movement-transition signature: Adams-Johnson moved from NAACP Youth Movement -> SNCC Tougaloo work -> Black Panther Party New York co-organizing across a 1962-1967 arc; the embedding catches that movement-transition trajectory as a coherent vector signature rather than as three distinct organizational phases the historiography often partitions, the same pattern visible in Junius Williams (entry 72) and Phil Hutchings (entry 98).
biographical_paragraph: While Adams-Johnson's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), her external biographical record is documented in the Civil Rights Digital Library and the Veterans of the Civil Rights Movement archive [src: 1, src: 2]. Adams-Johnson was born in Pocahontas, Mississippi to a sharecropping family, the fourth of seven children. Her first civil rights organizing came as a Brinkley High School student in a walkout protesting the beatings of Tougaloo College students who attempted to integrate Jackson's Woolworth lunch counter in 1963; she joined the NAACP's Jackson Youth Movement and served as its youth secretary [src: 2]. She enrolled at Tougaloo College in 1964 and worked with SNCC organizers on Mississippi voter-registration drives across her three years there. In 1967, after she was beaten during a protest, her mother sent her to live with her sister in White Plains, New York; she co-organized a White Plains chapter of the Black Panther Party there [src: 2]. She returned to academic work in the 1980s, became a college professor, and from the late 1990s has taught English at Jackson State University while continuing as a poet, speaker, and fiction writer [src: 2].
